hero.title: 🤝 Ethics
hero.subtitle: The intuitive mind is a sacred gift and the rational mind is a faithful servant - Albert Einstein

sections.hint: Focus on core medical ethics principles—especially patient autonomy, decision-making capacity, informed consent, confidentiality, end-of-life care, and surrogate decision-making.

topics.content:
• Core Principles: Autonomy (patient choice), Beneficence (do good), Non-maleficence (do no harm), Justice (fairness).
  • Autonomy: Competent patients have the right to accept or refuse any treatment, even life-saving care.
  • Decision-Making Capacity: Patient must understand, appreciate, reason, and communicate a choice.
  • Informed Consent: Requires disclosure, understanding, voluntariness, competence, and consent.
  • Exceptions to Consent: Emergency situations when patient is unable to consent and delay risks harm.
  • Surrogate Decision Makers: Used when patient lacks capacity; follow hierarchy (spouse → children → parents, etc.).
  • Substituted Judgment: Surrogate makes decisions based on what patient would have wanted.
  • Best Interest Standard: Used when patient wishes are unknown; choose what benefits patient most.
  • Advance Directives: Includes living wills and durable power of attorney for healthcare.
  • Living Will: Specifies patient preferences for end-of-life care.
  • Durable Power of Attorney: Assigns someone to make healthcare decisions.
  • Do Not Resuscitate (DNR): No CPR if cardiac arrest occurs; does not mean no treatment.
  • Brain Death: Irreversible loss of all brain function; legally equivalent to death.
  • Withdrawal of Care: Ethically equivalent to withholding care; both are acceptable.
  • Futility: Physicians are not obligated to provide non-beneficial treatments.
  • Confidentiality: Patient information must be protected unless exceptions apply.
  • Exceptions to Confidentiality: Harm to self or others, abuse reporting, communicable diseases.
  • Duty to Warn: Physician must warn identifiable third parties at risk (Tarasoff principle).
  • Mandatory Reporting: Child abuse, elder abuse, certain infections, and threats.
  • Minor Consent: Minors generally require parental consent except for STDs, pregnancy, contraception, and emergencies.
  • Emancipated Minor: Can make independent medical decisions.
  • Assent: Involve children in decisions when appropriate, even if not legally required.
  • End-of-Life Care: Focus on comfort, symptom control, and dignity.
  • Palliative Care: Symptom relief at any stage of illness.
  • Hospice Care: End-of-life care when life expectancy is ≤6 months.
  • Physician-Assisted Death: Legal only in certain jurisdictions; patient must self-administer.
  • Euthanasia: Illegal in most places; physician directly causes death.
  • Double Effect Principle: Giving medication (e.g., opioids) to relieve pain even if it may hasten death is acceptable.
  • Truth-Telling: Physicians should be honest; patients have right to know diagnosis and prognosis.
  • Therapeutic Privilege: Rarely used; withholding information only if disclosure causes serious harm.
  • Professional Boundaries: Avoid relationships that exploit patient trust.
  • Conflict of Interest: Must be disclosed and managed appropriately.
  • Research Ethics: Requires IRB approval, informed consent, and protection of subjects.
  • Belmont Principles: Respect for persons, beneficence, justice.
  • Clinical Trials: Phase I (safety), Phase II (efficacy), Phase III (comparison), Phase IV (post-marketing).
  • Placebo Use: Ethical only when no proven effective treatment exists.
  • Organ Donation: Requires consent (opt-in or opt-out systems depending on country).
  • Allocation of Resources: Based on justice and fairness, not social worth.
  • Impaired Physician: Must be reported if patient safety is at risk.
  • Key Concept: Ethics focuses on respecting patient autonomy while balancing beneficence, safety, and legal obligations.

1_medical_ethics.q: An 80-year-old man is admitted to the hospital with a massive intracranial bleed. He has been placed on a ventilator because of the respiratory failure associated with intracranial herniation. When you try to remove the ventilator, there are no respirations. The patient makes no purposeful movements. There is no pupilary reaction when you shine a light in his eyes. There is no nystagmus on cold caloric testing. Oculocephalic and corneal reflexes are absent. He left no specific wishes for his care. Which of the following is the most appropriate action regarding this patient?
a: Remove the ventilator.
Make the patient DNR.
Place a nasogastric tube to prevent aspiration.
Get a court order authorizing you to remove the ventilator.
Do an EEG (electroencephalogram) three times separated by six hours each time.
1_medical_ethics.l: The patient meets the clinical criteria for brain death: negative corneal reflex, no nystagmus to caloric stimulation, negative pupilary and oculocephalic reflexes, and the absence of spontaneous respiration when the ventilator is held. Brain death is the legal definition of death in the United States. There is no hope of recovery. When a patient is brain dead (dead by legal standard), you do not need to seek a court order or ethics committee approval prior to stopping all therapy. An EEG or cerebral blood flow study is not necessary to confirm death if the clinical brain-death criteria are met. The physician has the legal right to turn off the ventilator immediately, though discussing with the family first is strongly recommended.

1_medical_ethics.q: You are a resident in the emergency department. An irate parent comes to you furious because the social worker has been asking him about striking his child. The child is a 5-year-old boy who has been in the emergency department four times this year with several episodes of trauma that did not seem related. Today, the child is brought in with a complaint of "slipping into a hot bathtub" with a burn wound on his legs. The parent threatens to sue you and says "How dare you think that about me? I love my son!" What should you do?
a: Give reassurance to the parents and treat the patient's injury appropriately.
Ask risk management to evaluate the case.
Admit the child to remove him from the possibly dangerous environment.
Call the police.
Ask the father yourself if there has been any abuse.
Speak to the wife privately about possible episodes of abuse.
Explain to the parents that the next time this happens you will have to call child protective services.
Report the family to child protective services.
Give the parents a referral to a family therapist they can see with the child the following week.
1_medical_ethics.l: Reporting child abuse is mandatory even based on suspicion alone, which is strongly suggested by the child's history of multiple unrelated trauma and the suspicious burn injury. It doesn't matter what the parents say or if they deny the abuse; if you suspect abuse, you must report. The caregiver is legally protected from liability as long as the report is made honestly and without malice. You do not have the authority to remove the child (c). The police (d) are not appropriate for investigating child abuse; that is the role of Child Protective Services.

1_medical_ethics.q: You are working at the desk in your hospital when another employee of the hospital asks for information about a patient who was admitted last night with a pulmonary embolus secondary to cancer. You know the details of the case. The person requesting the information states that he is a close friend and co-worker of your patient. He shows you proper identification proving he really is a co-worker of your patient who also works in the hospital. Which of the following is the most appropriate response to this request?
a: Give him the information on the patient.
Give him the information only if he is a relative of the patient.
Inform him that you are not at liberty to give details regarding the patient without the patient's permission.
Have him sign a release or consent form before revealing the information.
1_medical_ethics.l: Confidentiality is a fundamental right of all patients. You must refuse to release any specific medical information without direct permission from the patient. This is true even if the person asking is a co-worker, superior, friend, or family member, unless they are directly involved in the patient's care. You must have the patient—not the person requesting the information—sign the consent form.

1_medical_ethics.q: You are seeing patients in clinic when two men in dark suits and dark glasses come in and show you badges marking them as members of a federal law enforcement agency. The identification is legitimate. These "men in black" inform you that they are making a "minor investigation" of one of your patients. They ask to look at the patient's chart for a few minutes, saying, "You wouldn't want to interfere with a federal investigation, would you?" What should you do?
a: Give them the chart.
Give them the chart but watch what they do with it.
Ask them to sign a release for the chart so you are absolved of responsibility.
Tell them you cannot show them the chart unless there is a signed release from the patient.
Tell them that you can give copies but not the original record.
Don't give them the chart but read the relevant information to them.
1_medical_ethics.l: A patient's medical records cannot be released without a clear, signed release from the patient or a court order (search warrant or subpoena). The fact that they are federal agents does not give them the automatic right to enter your "property" (the medical record) or access confidential information without legal authorization.

1_medical_ethics.q: You are a psychiatrist in session with a patient who tells you he thinks his boss at work is persecuting him. The patient has had mild schizophrenia. The patient asks you if you can keep a secret and then tells you that he is planning to kill his boss "when the time is right." You say, "Of course, everything you tell me during the session will always be confidential." What should you do?
a: Keep the patient's session confidential but make attempts to discourage the patient from his plan.
Inform your medical director and let him handle it.
Inform law enforcement agencies of the threat to the patient's boss.
Inform the patient's boss that he is in danger.
Inform both the patient's boss as well as law enforcement of the threat.
1_medical_ethics.l: A patient's right to confidentiality ends where a third person's right to safety begins. This is an example of the Tarasoff principle (Duty to Warn). When a mentally ill patient discloses an intent to harm an identifiable victim, the physician has an absolute mandate to inform both law enforcement and the potential victim.

1_medical_ethics.q: You are discussing the care of an elderly woman with her family. Although she is awake and alert, the patient is very ill and physically fragile. You are awaiting the results of a biopsy for what will likely be cancer, which has already metastasized throughout the body. The family asks that you inform them first about the results of the biopsy. They are very loving and caring and are constantly surrounding the patient. They do not want to depress the patient further, and because there will be no hope for a cure they see no reason to ruin her remaining life with this information. What should you tell them?
a: You will honor their wishes.
You agree with their wishes and you ask them to give you the necessary written request.
You ask them to involve the ethics committee for the hospital.
You tell them that you are obligated to inform the patient of all the findings.
Explain to them that that decision can only be made by the health-care proxy.
1_medical_ethics.l: The first duty is to keep the patient fully informed about their health care. Unless there is significant evidence of possible psychological harm (e.g., the patient is actively suicidal), the physician has an absolute duty to the patient first, not the family. The patient has decision-making capacity (awake and alert), so their right to know their own information overrules the family's wishes.

1_medical_ethics.q: Your patient has just recently been diagnosed with familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP). This disorder is chronic, progressive, and fatal. There is a genetic test that can tell whether children of parents with the disease will develop it. The test is very accurate. The patient has become divorced and refuses to give you his consent to inform his ex-wife who now has custody of their three children. He threatens to sue you if you reveal elements of his medical care to his ex-wife. What should you do?
a: Respect the patient's right to confidentiality.
Transfer the patient's care to another physician as long as the patient agrees.
Ask the health department to inform the patient's ex-wife about the disease risk.
Seek a court order to inform the patient's ex-wife.
Inform the patient's ex-wife of the risk to the children.
Inform the ex-wife's doctor.
1_medical_ethics.l: The patient's right to confidentiality ends where it conflicts with the safety of other people. FAP is a very specific intervention (screening for polyps at age 12, with colon cancer prevention via colectomy if polyps are found) that can prevent harm to the children. The mother, as the caregiver, has a right to know about the health-care issues affecting her children so she can plan for their future, and the right to safety supersedes the father's right to privacy in this case. This disclosure does not require a court order.

1_medical_ethics.q: You are seeing a patient in clinic who has developed tuberculosis. He is an undocumented (illegal) immigrant. His family will need to be screened for tuberculosis with PPD skin testing. He is frightened of being deported if the Department of Health learns his immigration status. What should you tell him?
a: "Don't worry, the Department of Health does not ask or report immigration status."
"Only people who are noncompliant with medications are reported to the government."
"Don't worry, I will fully treat you before we deport you."
"I am sorry but there is nothing I can do about it; there is mandatory reporting to the government."
1_medical_ethics.l: The physician's ethical duty is to provide medical care to all patients. Neither physicians nor the Department of Health act as agents to report a person's immigration status to the government. There is no mandatory reporting of immigration status. Mandatory reporting for TB focuses on communicable disease control (public health), not deportation or criminal justice.

1_medical_ethics.q: A couple comes to see you after having tried in-vitro fertilization and artificial insemination. They are very happy because they have recently been successful in giving birth to a child. They have a significant amount of leftover sperm, eggs, and some fertilized gametes/embryos and they are thinking about selling them. What should you tell them?
a: It is legal to sell only the eggs.
It is legal to sell only the sperm.
It is legal to sell both the sperm and eggs but not the embryos.
It is illegal to sell any of them.
It is legal to sell all of them.
1_medical_ethics.l: It is legal to sell and donate unfertilized gametes such as sperm and eggs. A fertilized gamete or embryo can only be donated, not sold. Payment for solid organs and non-renewable tissues is generally unethical/illegal.

1_medical_ethics.q: You are a fourth-year medical student with a patient who has been in a severe motor vehicle accident. The patient has a subdural hematoma that led to cerebral herniation before it could be drained. Over the last few days, the patient has lost all brainstem reflexes and is now brain dead. You have the closest relationship with the family of anyone on the team. The ventilator is to be removed soon and organ donation is considered. Who should ask for consent for organ donation in this case?
a: You, because you are the person with the best relationship with the family
The resident because you are only a student
Attending of record
Hospital administration
Organ-donor network
1_medical_ethics.l: The medical team caring for the patient must not be the ones to ask for organ donation, as this creates a conflict of interest. When a caregiver asks, it can lead the family to believe the team is prioritizing organ harvesting over the patient's life. The Organ-Donor Network has trained personnel who are legally mandated to obtain consent and have a significantly higher success rate.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 50-year-old male gambler owes money to everyone and is seeking a rapid source of cash. He answers an advertisement in a foreign newspaper offering $30,000 for one of his kidneys. He comes to see you for medical evaluation prior to the approval to be a donor. What should you tell him?
a: It is never acceptable to receive any money for solid organs or bone marrow.
It is okay as long as the donor's remaining kidney is healthy.
It is acceptable as long as the recipient truly need the organ.
It is acceptable if the surgery for both the donation as well at the implantation in the recipient is occurring in a foreign country.
Reimbursement for cost of travel and lodging for the donation is acceptable, but profiting from the donation is unacceptable.
1_medical_ethics.l: Payment for solid organs (profiting) is ethically unacceptable and illegal in the United States. Selling organs would favor the wealthiest and not those most in need. However, it is acceptable to reimburse the donor for costs directly related to the donation, such as travel, lodging, or lost wages, to remove a disincentive to donation.

1_medical_ethics.q: A man arrives at the emergency department on a ventilator after an accident. He is brain dead by all criteria. He has an organ-donor card in his wallet indicating his desire to donate. The organ-donor team contacts the family. The family refuses to sign consent for the donation. What should be done?
a: Remove the organs anyway.
Wait for the patient's heart to stop and then remove the organs.
Stop the ventilator and remove the organs.
Seek a court order to overrule the family.
Honor the wishes of the family; no donation.
1_medical_ethics.l: An organ donor card is an indication of the patient's wishes but is not fully binding. In practice, and in most jurisdictions, family objection can overrule the wishes expressed on an organ donor card. If the family's wishes could be overruled, there would be no point in asking for consent.

1_medical_ethics.q: You have a patient with severe multiple sclerosis that is advanced and progressive who now develops renal failure secondary to diabetes. The patient is alert and has elected to put the DNR order in place at her own discretion. The patient's potassium level is now markedly elevated at $8~meq/L$. Which of the following is the most appropriate management of this patient?
a: Dialysis cannot be done because of the DNR order.
You can do the dialysis if the DNR is reversed for the procedure.
Go ahead with the dialysis; ignore the DNR order.
Give kayexalate until the DNR status is discussed with the family.
1_medical_ethics.l: A "Do Not Resuscitate" (DNR) order is specifically defined as refusing cardiopulmonary resuscitative efforts (chest compressions, electrical cardioversion, etc.) in the event of death. A DNR order has nothing to do with other forms of care the patient is receiving, such as dialysis, surgery, or ICU admission. The patient is alert and competent; hyperkalemia is life-threatening. Therefore, you should proceed with the indicated, non-resuscitative medical treatment (dialysis) immediately.

1_medical_ethics.q: A patient from a skilled-nursing facility is admitted to the hospital for severe upper gastrointestinal bleeding. The patient has a DNR order in place. The bleeding has not stopped despite multiple transfusions, octreotide, proton pump inhibitors, and endoscopy. He needs monitoring and evaluation in an intensive care unit. What should you do?
a: Reverse the DNR order and transfer to the ICU as needed.
You cannot be DNR and be in the ICU.
Transfer to the ICU as needed; okay to be DNR in the ICU.
He can go to the ICU with the DNR but cannot go to surgery.
He can go to the ICU with the DNR but cannot be intubated.
1_medical_ethics.l: DNR means "no CPR" if the patient dies. It is not a generalized limitation on all forms of therapy. DNR orders are often confused with generalized withholding of care such as transfusion or ICU management. DNR orders do not automatically mean the patient is terminal. Patients can still undergo surgery or be placed in the intensive care unit if they are DNR.

1_medical_ethics.q: A man is admitted for acute appendicitis. He is elderly and has had a home DNR order in place since he had a previous admission for another reason last year. He needs urgent surgery for the appendicitis. He still wishes to be DNR. What should you do about the surgery?
a: No surgery can be done with a patient who is DNR.
Reverse the DNR order for the surgery.
DNR is acceptable only if the surgery does not require intubation.
Surgery is acceptable while DNR if an additional consent is signed.
DNR does not preclude surgery; proceed with the appendectomy.
1_medical_ethics.l: DNR does not limit treatment other than resuscitative efforts. DNR is not a contraindication to surgery. You do not need to reverse the order or obtain additional consent. The team is still expected to provide aggressive management for the patient's medical problems, such as an acute, life-threatening appendicitis.

1_medical_ethics.q: An 84-year-old woman is seen by you in clinic for routine management of her multiple medical problems. She comes alone and you notice that she has been losing weight and has several contusions. She is a widow and lives with her granddaughter and her granddaughter's husband who recently lost his job. On questioning she tells you that her granddaughter's husband does occasionally strike her when he is drunk. What should you do?
a: Report the abuse only at her specific request.
Arrange for a meeting with your patient and the grandson-in-law.
Remove the patient from the home and place her in an adult home.
Have a home health aid placed to keep an eye on the patient.
Arrange for an order of protection from the police.
Report the grandson-in-law's abuse to adult protective service.
Speak directly to the grandson-in-law about the problem.
1_medical_ethics.l: The ethical requirement of physicians to report elder abuse is clear and uncompromising. The reporting of elder abuse does not depend on the wishes of the patient. You must act to protect the patient from an environment that might kill her, even if she objects due to fear or other reasons. The correct agency is Adult Protective Services.

1_medical_ethics.q: You are the staff physician in a state penitentiary in a state where capital punishment is legal. An execution is in process and the warden calls you because the technician is unable to start the intravenous line that is necessary for the lethal injection. The warden wants you to start the line and supervise the pharmacist. What should you tell him?
a: "No problem, I will start the line right away."
"I can start the line, but I cannot push the medications."
"I can start the line, but I cannot prepare or inject the medications."
"I am sorry; I cannot participate in the execution at all as a physician."
"I can take care of all of it."
1_medical_ethics.l: The ethical guidelines of the AMA and ACP are expressly clear: a physician cannot participate in "any action which would directly cause the death of the condemned or assist another individual to directly cause the death of the condemned." This prohibition includes starting the IV line, mixing or formulating medications, administering medications, or even giving technical advice. Physician participation directly opposes the ethical imperative to preserve life.

1_medical_ethics.q: You are preparing a clinical trial of different doses of a certain medication. This medication has already been proven to be clinically effective and is already approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA). You are only studying to see whether a higher dose of the medication will lead to enhanced benefit. Which of the following is true concerning your study?
a: Institutional review board (IRB) approval is not necessary.
Informed consent is required to participate.
Informed consent is not required because the medication has already been FDA-approved.
Informed consent is not required because you are trying to demonstrate benefit, not harm.
1_medical_ethics.l: All clinical trials require informed consent. Experimental subjects have an absolute right to know they are participating in a trial. This is true even if the treatment only offers benefit and has no adverse effects. The principle of autonomy means you cannot do something for someone without their consent, even if it helps them.

1_medical_ethics.q: Which of the following most accurately describes the participation of prisoners in clinical trials and research?
a: There is no need for informed consent.
No monetary compensation may occur.
Prisoners are not permitted to participate as research subjects.
Their rights are identical to a nonprisoner.
Research on prisoners is always considered unethical.
Participation in research is an accepted means of winning early release from prison.
1_medical_ethics.l: Prisoners have the same rights in terms of participation in clinical research as non-prisoners. They retain the right to informed consent and the right to refuse participation. They are also entitled to monetary compensation. Participation cannot be used as a criterion for shortening a prison sentence.

1_medical_ethics.q: You are in the process of finalizing the results of your research for publication. You are the principal investigator of a clinical trial studying the effects of HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors on cardiac mortality. A prominent manufacturer of one of these medications provided major funding for the study. Which of the following is the most accurate in an ethical preparation for authorship of the publication?
a: Accepting money from a company prohibits you from being listed as an author.
Funding source has no impact on publication requirements.
You can be listed as the author after the institutional review board checks the paper for evidence of bias.
There are no restrictions on your authorship as long as you disclose the financial affiliation.
There are no requirements as long as the data are accurate.
You do not have to do anything as long as the checks were written to the institution and not to you.
1_medical_ethics.l: There is no automatic ethical difficulty with industry sponsorship of clinical trials, and financial support does not eliminate you as an author. The absolute requirement for all authors is to disclose all financial or business affiliations at the time of publication. This is essential to ensure an objective interpretation of the research data by allowing the audience to judge for possible bias.

1_medical_ethics.q: Which of the following most accurately describes the primary role of an institutional review board (IRB)?
a: Protect the institution from liability
Protect the physician from liability
Ensure the ethical and humane treatment of human subjects in experimentation
Ensure the study is scientifically accurate
Ensure that the study is financially feasible for the institution
1_medical_ethics.l: The IRB's primary function is to assure the ethical integrity of clinical trials. This includes ensuring adequate informed consent, assessing that the research question is valid, and making sure the subjects do not suffer purposeful harm for the sake of collecting data. The IRB's function is to ensure that the care of the subject is more important than the collection of data.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 35-year-old female patient comes to your office with a large form to be filled out certifying that her health is within normal limits. This is as a part of pre-employment evaluation and is required in order for the patient to get the job. The form also asks for the results of the patient's APC (adenomatous polyposis coli) gene. This is in order for the company to determine which of its long-term employees will need expensive care. What should be your response?
a: Perform the test.
Perform the test, but do not share the results with the employer.
Do not perform the test.
Ask the patient if she wants the test performed and the results reported.
Perform the colonoscopy; the employer is entitled to know about current health problems but not future ones.
Include the test only if the patient has family members with colon cancer.
1_medical_ethics.l: Employers do not have a right to confidential health-care information. You may only perform and report tests to the employer at the request of the patient. Genetic testing is generally not yet clear in terms of clinical impact and has the potential to lead to discrimination, which physicians have an ethical duty to prevent. The correct action is to place the autonomy of the patient first and let her decide.

1_medical_ethics.q: You have a patient with a strong family history of breast cancer. As a matter of the patient's request, you perform a BRCA genetic test to see if there is an increased risk for breast cancer. The patient's employer is requesting a copy of any genetic testing that may have been done. What should you do?
a: Give the employer the information.
Refer the employer to the hospital lawyer.
Bring the request to the ethics committee.
Refuse to provide the information to the employer.
Give the information if it is positive on a repeat exam.
1_medical_ethics.l: The employer has no right to a patient's genetic information. Genetic information (like all medical information) is confidential. Genetic tests for breast cancer risk do not impact job performance or job safety, which are the only forms of information an employer might have a claim to.

1_medical_ethics.q: You are a resident invited to a dinner given by a pharmaceutical company. In addition to the dinner there is a lecture given on a medical subject as well as a $500 gift certificate to a department store for attending the presentation. Which of the following is the most appropriate action in this case?
a: It is entirely unethical to accept any of it; refuse everything.
It is only ethical to attend the lecture.
Do not accept the money, but the dinner and the lecture are ethically acceptable.
You may accept all three components.
Cash payments from industry are acceptable as long as they are not tied to prescribing specific medications.
You can accept because you are a resident and not an attending.
1_medical_ethics.l: Modest meals (dinner) associated with educational events (lecture) are generally ethically acceptable. Direct monetary payments (gift certificate to a department store) or gifts purely for entertainment (theater tickets, ski trips) are prohibited. Industry gifts are presumed to be an attempt to buy influence.

1_medical_ethics.q: You have been asked to give Grand Rounds at a hospital. A manufacturer of a new medical device is sponsoring you. In exchange, you are being offered a $1,000 fee for your talk. What should you do?
a: You must refuse the money.
You may accept only as long as you donate it to charity.
You may accept as long as you don't reveal the honorarium to the audience.
It is permissible to take the money as long as you disclose any other financial or business connection with this or any other company.
You may accept the money as long as you submit the content of your talk to an independent panel for review to ensure objectivity.
1_medical_ethics.l: Speakers can accept a cash honorarium for preparing and giving a lecture. The mandatory ethical requirement is that the speaker disclose all financial participation with industry. This allows the audience to judge for potential bias. There is no requirement to donate the fee to charity or to submit the content for prior review. 📄 Medical Ethics Practice Questions (Set 2 of 4: Questions 26–50)

1_medical_ethics.q: A patient admitted with palpitations from a panic attack is somewhat hypomanic with pressured speech, anxiety, and flight of ideas. After several days, he's feeling much better and is extremely grateful for your help. He is quite wealthy and he offers to borrow five million dollars from the bank "to help you with your research." What should you tell him?
a: "Thank you very much, but I cannot accept."
"I accept!"
"I accept, but only for a study of bipolar disorder."
"I would be happy to take your money, but I must have you evaluated by psychiatry first."
1_medical_ethics.l: You cannot accept gifts from patients who clearly show evidence of an acute psychiatric disturbance. Even if the patient isn't ill enough to be legally deemed incompetent, gifts given by patients must be truly objective and free from any question of acute emotional duress leading to the gift. The appropriate response is to politely refuse the gift.

1_medical_ethics.q: You have a patient in your clinic who is an elderly woman with multiple medical problems. Her family is extremely grateful for your care and they bring you a meal they cooked at home, a cake, and a scarf. What should you do?
a: Accept the gift but report it.
Accept the gift.
Offer payment for the food.
Refuse the gift.
It is ethical to accept the gift if you share the food with the rest of the house staff.
Accept the food but not the scarf.
1_medical_ethics.l: Small gifts from patients of nominal or modest value are ethically acceptable. This includes food, plants, and small articles of clothing such as a scarf. Refusing such tokens of gratitude, which are signs of a good doctor/patient relationship, would be hurtful. Gifts must not be tied to a specific expectation of care. There is no reporting or disclosure requirement for small gifts of nominal value.

1_medical_ethics.q: You have been invited to participate in a "Medical Jeopardy" game sponsored by a pharmaceutical manufacturer. The winners are to receive $100 gift certificates to the medical school's bookstore. All of the participants are to receive a stethoscope. The audience and all the participants are offered a free meal. Which of the following is most appropriate to accept?
a: All the gifts.
Only the meal.
Only the stethoscope.
The meal and stethoscope, but not the gift certificate.
None of the gifts are acceptable.
1_medical_ethics.l: Acceptable gifts from industry include: Modest meals associated with educational experiences (like a medical game or lecture). Gifts with direct educational or medical value that enhance patient care, such as a stethoscope. Gift certificates that are restricted to a medical school bookstore (since this is deemed educational/medical). Gifts should generally be small (less than $100 in value). Prohibited items include cash, entertainment (like tickets), or gift certificates to non-medical department stores.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 32-year-old pregnant woman comes to your prenatal clinic. She has a history of syphilis and gonorrhea in the past but her VDRL/RPR is negative now. An HIV test is offered as a routine part of her prenatal evaluation as well as because of the history of previous STDs. You explain to her the importance of the test for her baby's well-being. She refuses the test when offered. What should you do?
a: No test; she has the right to refuse.
PCR RNA viral load testing as an alternative.
Consent for HIV testing is not needed in pregnancy because it is to protect the health of the baby.
Add the test to the other routine tests that are to be drawn.
Administer empiric antiretroviral therapy to prevent perinatal transmission.
HIV testing is now part of routine prenatal care and no specific consent is needed.
1_medical_ethics.l: Patients cannot be tested for HIV against their specific refusal. While HIV testing should be offered universally in pregnancy, it is not mandatory. The patient's autonomy (right to refuse treatment or testing for herself) legally outweighs concerns about the fetus's safety, even though medications could prevent transmission. HIV testing requires an additional layer of consent beyond routine blood work.

1_medical_ethics.q: You have a patient who is an HIV-positive physician. He has recently found out that he is HIV-positive. He is very concerned about confidentiality and you are the only one who knows he is HIV-positive. He asks you who you are legally obligated to inform. What should you tell him?
a: His insurance company
State government
His patients
His patients, only if he performs a procedure such as surgery where transmission can occur
No one without his direct written consent
His employer
The hospital human resources department
1_medical_ethics.l: There is no duty on the part of an HIV-positive health-care worker to inform patients of their HIV status. HIV-positive individuals have a right to privacy, and you have no mandatory obligation to inform the state, insurance, employer, or his patients. Universal precautions are the standard mechanism to protect patients. Disclosure of HIV status requires an additional consent.

1_medical_ethics.q: You have an HIV-positive patient in need of drainage of a dental abscess. There is a dentist in your multispecialty group practice. He knows you are an infectious diseases/HIV specialist. Your office manager tells you the dentist and his staff are "scared of your HIV-positive patients" and they don't want to see them. What is your response?
a: This is illegal; the dentist is liable and can be sued.
You request the medical director and have him instruct the dentist to see the patient.
You prescribe the use of oral antibiotics alone instead.
Refer your patient to another dentist outside of your group practice.
1_medical_ethics.l: Refusing to treat an HIV-positive patient solely because of their status is considered unethical. However, you cannot compel a physician (or dentist) to take on a patient if they do not voluntarily consent to the doctor–patient relationship. Since a surgical drainage is needed, oral antibiotics alone are insufficient. The most appropriate action is to ensure the patient receives care by referring them to another provider who will perform the procedure.

1_medical_ethics.q: You are evaluating a new patient that has come to your clinic for management of his HIV infection. He has high CD4 cells and a low viral load and does not require treatment. He is married and does not practice safe sex with his wife. His wife is unaware of her husband's HIV status. What is your duty?
a: Encourage discussion between the husband and wife and strongly suggest he disclose his HIV status to his wife.
You must keep his HIV status entirely confidential at all times.
Have the city Department of Health inform the wife.
Send the wife a letter to come to your office because of an "urgent health issue" and then inform her yourself.
There is no need for partner notification as long as they are practicing safe sex.
1_medical_ethics.l: You have a duty both to protect patient confidentiality and the safety of innocent third parties. The first step is always to counsel the patient to notify their partners voluntarily. Direct physician or health department notification is only appropriate if the patient is unwilling or unable to notify their partner.

1_medical_ethics.q: You have an HIV-positive patient in the office. You ask her if she has informed her partner that she is HIV-positive. She has repeatedly resisted your attempts to have her inform the partner. She is pregnant with his child. The partner is in the waiting room and you have met him many times. What should you do?
a: Inform the partner now.
Respect her confidentiality.
Refer your patient to another physician who is comfortable with her wishes.
Tell the partner to practice safe sex from now on but don't tell him her HIV status.
1_medical_ethics.l: When a patient refuses to inform an identifiable, specific, and currently at-risk partner, the duty to protect the safety of the innocent person (the Tarasoff principle) supersedes confidentiality. Since the patient has repeatedly refused and the partner is present and at high risk (implied unprotected sex due to pregnancy), the physician has legal immunity to carry out the notification and a duty to warn to avoid liability if the partner is harmed.
1_medical_ethics.h: HIV Partner Notification (Refusal, Pregnant Partner Present)

1_medical_ethics.q: An 87-year-old man comes to see you for follow-up to a minor concussion sustained a few weeks ago. He was in a minor motor vehicle accident in which his head hit the dashboard but he did not lose consciousness. Your patient has glaucoma and presbycusis. He renewed his driver’s license by mail two years ago and the only restriction is that he must wear glasses. You are uncertain whether the patient should be driving. What should you do?
a: Inform the patient’s family and explain to them that they should not allow the patient to continue driving because it is dangerous for him.
MRI of the brain.
Neurology evaluation.
Start Aricept to improve his memory.
Discuss the issue with the patient and encourage him to find alternate methods of transportation.
Remove his license from him.
Mandatory reporting to the police.
1_medical_ethics.l: Laws for reporting impaired drivers due to age or cognitive impairment vary widely by state, and there is no national standard. Since the exam is national, the universally agreed-upon answer is to prioritize counseling and encouraging the patient to curtail driving or seek alternate transportation. Physicians cannot suspend driving privileges or remove a license — only the DMV can do that.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 37-year-old man has seemed severely depressed for some time but refuses to discuss either his feelings or treatment options of any kind. He firmly denies suicidal ideation. You prescribe a serotonin reuptake inhibitor for him and tell him that it is a vitamin. Over the next several months, his mood markedly improves and he feels much better. Which of the following most appropriately characterizes your action toward the patient?
a: Your action is appropriate because it benefited the patient.
Your action is appropriate because there were no side effects.
Your action is not appropriate because you are not a psychiatrist.
Your action is not appropriate because you treated the patient without his consent.
Your action is appropriate because you are sincerely trying to help the patient.
Your action is acceptable as long as you inform the patient now that he is feeling better.
1_medical_ethics.l: Patient autonomy is considered more important and takes precedence over beneficence (doing good). You cannot treat a competent adult against his will, even if the treatment only benefits him, has no adverse effects, and is done with sincerity. Treating a patient without consent, especially through deception, is legally equivalent to assault and battery.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 67-year-old woman is diagnosed with breast cancer. She is fully alert and very specifically both verbally and in writing tells you that she does not want to have surgery. Over the next several weeks, the patient becomes confused and loses the capacity to understand the details of her medical care. The husband and son now approach you to perform the surgery. Which of the following is most appropriate?
a: Refuse to do surgery and follow the original advance directive.
Perform the surgery only if the request is in writing.
Honor the family’s request and perform the surgery.
Consult the ethics committee.
Ask the family to seek a court order.
Perform the surgery only if you really believe that it will benefit the patient in the long term.
1_medical_ethics.l: Decisions made when a patient is competent are valid when capacity is lost. The last known wishes of the patient, whether written or verbal, must be respected. The patient’s prior decision (clear refusal of surgery) is considered an advance directive and overrules the family’s later request and the physician’s desire to treat.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 52-year-old man with cerebral palsy is being evaluated for screening colonoscopy. He has a mental age of 8 and a second-grade reading level in terms of comprehension. You have thoroughly explained the procedure to him in terms of risks and benefits. He repeatedly refuses the procedure entirely on the basis of “I just don’t want it.” What should you do?
a: Perform the procedure.
Seek consent from the family.
Honor his decision and do not do the colonoscopy.
Seek a court order mandating the procedure.
1_medical_ethics.l: A patient’s inability to manage other areas of life (like finances or living independently) does not automatically mean they lack capacity to consent to or refuse medical procedures. The standard for capacity to refuse medical procedures is a very low threshold. If the patient can understand the procedure, risks, and benefits, their refusal must be honored. Forcing the procedure against his will is legally and ethically unacceptable.

1_medical_ethics.q: Mr. M. consents to a procedure on his left ear. After the patient is anesthetized, Dr. W. discovers that the right ear is in greater need of surgery. What should the surgeon do?
a: Perform the procedure on the right ear if it is clear that it is more necessary.
Wake the patient up and seek consent for a different procedure.
Seek a second opinion from another surgeon and proceed with the more necessary procedure.
Perform the procedure on both ears.
1_medical_ethics.l: Consent must be obtained for each specific procedure. Consent for one procedure (left ear surgery) cannot infer consent for another (right ear surgery or a different procedure found after opening). Neither medical necessity nor assumed patient benefit is sufficient to override the need for explicit informed consent. The patient must be awakened and informed to make an autonomous decision.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 52-year-old Spanish-speaking woman has arrived for the first day of a clinical trial of chemotherapy for breast cancer. You suddenly remember the need for signing a consent form. You ask a medical student to “get the consent.” He walks up to the patient and says in English, “Sign this,” and she signs. She completes the trial but her hair falls out and she files suit against you for an improper informed consent. Why will this lawsuit be successful?
a: The risks of the treatment were not explained.
The explanation was not in a language you were sure she could understand.
She experienced harm from the study medication.
Someone who didn’t understand the study obtained the consent.
All of the above.
1_medical_ethics.l: For consent to be valid, several criteria were violated, all contributing to the success of the lawsuit: Risks/Benefits: The risks of the treatment (hair loss) and the study goal must be explained. Language: The explanation must be in a language the patient understands. Using a translator is required for language barriers. Qualified Person: The person who obtains consent must be qualified and knowledgeable enough to explain the risks, benefits, and alternatives. The hair loss provides the tangible harm element necessary for a malpractice suit.

1_medical_ethics.q: An unconscious man is brought to the emergency department for a motor vehicle accident hemorrhaging profusely, hypotensive and stuporous. You have never met the patient before and no one at your institution knows him. He is wearing a T-shirt that says, “Kiss me, I’m a Jehovah’s Witness.” What should you do about the blood transfusion?
a: Give the blood.
Wait for him to awaken enough to sign consent.
Wait for the family.
Seek a court order.
Give intravenous fluids alone.
1_medical_ethics.l: In an emergency where the patient is unconscious and unable to speak for himself, consent is implied. You must give the necessary lifesaving treatment immediately. A T-shirt is not a clear, legally binding advance directive. Since you have no clear evidence of his wishes and the situation is life-threatening, waiting for him to awaken, waiting for the family, or seeking a court order is incorrect as it would delay lifesaving care.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 34-year-old man is brought to the emergency department with fever, headache, and a change in mental status leading to significant disorientation. He is in need of an urgent lumbar puncture and intravenous antibiotics. He is agitated and is waving off anyone who tries to get near him. What should you do?
a: Sedate the patient with lorazepam and perform the lumbar puncture.
Wait for the family to obtain consent.
Use blood cultures as an alternative.
MRI of the brain.
Ask the co-workers to sign consent.
1_medical_ethics.l: This is an urgent, life-threatening medical emergency (possible meningitis). The patient is disoriented, meaning he lacks the capacity to make an informed decision or refusal. In emergencies with a non-competent adult and no proxy available, physicians must act in the patient’s best interest. Sedating him to safely perform the essential diagnostic procedure (LP) and administer life-saving antibiotics is appropriate.

1_medical_ethics.q: A man is suffering from profound delirium from fever and has lost capacity to understand his problems. His brother is his health-care proxy. The patient is a Jehovah’s Witness and is adamantly opposed to transfusion even if he dies, and the brother is not opposed. The patient has clearly told both you and the brother this in the past. The patient’s brother believes that the proscription against transfusions is “barbaric and stupid.” What should the patient’s brother do?
a: Arrange a Jehovah’s Witness meeting to discuss the situation.
Ask their parents to make the decision.
Ask the doctor to make the decision.
He should not give consent for the transfusion.
He should give consent for transfusion.
1_medical_ethics.l: A Health-Care Proxy’s role is to carry out the patient’s clearly expressed wishes, not substitute personal judgment. Because the patient explicitly refused transfusions while competent, those instructions must be honored. The proxy’s disagreement or belief that the refusal is unreasonable is irrelevant; the proxy must communicate the patient’s choice, not override it.
1_medical_ethics.h: Health-Care Proxy (Disagreement with Patient’s Prior Wish)

1_medical_ethics.q: Mr. Sakiewiec is a 32-year-old man with severe mental retardation who has been institutionalized since childhood. He is noncommunicative and has never been able to verbalize his preferences. He has developed severe, incurable leukemia. Chemotherapy involves significant risk and discomfort and only a small chance of prolonging survival. What should be done in terms of his medical treatment?
a: Proceed with the chemotherapy.
Confer an “expert panel” to determine therapy.
Bone marrow transplantation.
Ask the guardian what is in the best interests of the patient.
1_medical_ethics.l: For a patient who has never been competent, there can be no prior directive or substituted judgment. Decisions must follow the best-interests standard, meaning what a reasonable person would consider most beneficial for the patient’s welfare and comfort. The legally appointed guardian provides this consent based on compassion and medical guidance.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 27-year-old pregnant woman in her last trimester presents with severe cephalopelvic disproportion. Physicians recommend a cesarean section. She understands the procedure but refuses it, saying she does not wish to undergo surgery, even after learning the fetus may not survive childbirth. What should you do?
a: Honor her wishes and do not perform the C-section.
Psychiatry evaluation.
Sedate her and perform the surgery.
Obtain a court order to perform the surgery.
Explain the situation to the baby’s father and ask him for consent.
1_medical_ethics.l: A competent adult retains absolute autonomy over her own body, even during pregnancy. The fetus does not possess independent legal rights before birth. Therefore, the mother may refuse any medical or surgical intervention, even if the refusal endangers the fetus. The father has no legal authority to consent to procedures on the mother’s body.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 52-year-old man sees you in follow-up after radical prostatectomy. He had been fully informed about the risks such as incontinence and impotence. Neither occurred. Later he discovers online that non-surgical options, like implantation of radioactive seeds, existed. He sues for improper informed consent. What will be the most likely outcome of the suit?
a: He will lose because there were no adverse effects.
He will lose because you explained the risks before consent.
He will lose because radical prostatectomy is standard.
He will win because you did not inform him of the risks and benefits of alternative therapy to surgery.
He will win because radioactive seeds are superior.
1_medical_ethics.l: True informed consent requires disclosure of all reasonable alternatives, including non-surgical options. Failure to present choices deprives the patient of autonomy, even if the procedure was successful. The omission invalidates the consent, making the lawsuit valid despite the absence of complications.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 58-year-old man traveling in New York suffers a myocardial infarction and deteriorates. He is intubated and disoriented. His wife, the designated health-care proxy, is in another city. You need her consent for coronary bypass surgery. Which of the following is true?
a: She must come to the hospital to sign consent in person.
The wife must designate a local guardian until her arrival.
You must repeat the angioplasty instead.
Telephone consent is only valid for minor procedures.
Take consent for the bypass over the phone and have a second person witness the telephone consent.
1_medical_ethics.l: Telephone consent is legally valid if obtained from an authorized surrogate (e.g., spouse, proxy). It applies equally to minor or major procedures. A second staff member must witness the call and document the discussion to ensure proper verification and reduce liability.
1_medical_ethics.h: Informed Consent (Telephone Consent for Major Surgery)

1_medical_ethics.q: Mr. Dorone, a 22-year-old man, sustained a subdural hematoma and needs blood transfusion for lifesaving surgery. His parents refuse the transfusion based on their religious beliefs. What should be done?
a: Honor the parents’ wishes and do not give the blood.
Give the blood.
Wait for a formal court hearing.
1_medical_ethics.l: The patient is an adult (22 years old). Parents cannot consent or refuse care on behalf of adult children. Since he is unconscious and unable to consent, and the treatment is emergent and lifesaving, implied consent applies. You must transfuse, regardless of parental objection.
1_medical_ethics.h: Informed Consent (Parent Refusing Lifesaving Blood for Adult Child)

1_medical_ethics.q: A 27-year-old Jehovah’s Witness in labor begins hemorrhaging severely. She has clearly stated and signed a refusal of blood transfusion, acknowledging she may die. When she loses consciousness, her non-Witness husband asks that blood be given and says he will sign consent. What should you tell him?
a: You will honor his wish if he is the proxy.
You will honor his wish and give the blood.
You will give the blood only if the patient is unconscious.
You cannot give the blood.
1_medical_ethics.l: A competent patient’s prior decisions remain valid after capacity is lost. Her written refusal constitutes an advance directive, which the husband cannot overrule. Even if death is imminent, her autonomy and prior explicit refusal must be respected.
1_medical_ethics.h: Informed Consent (Advance Directive Refusing Blood for Self)

1_medical_ethics.q: A resident writes an order for Diovan 10 mg, but due to sloppy handwriting, the nurse and pharmacy administer digoxin 10 mg daily. Three days later, the patient develops a hemodynamically unstable rhythm disturbance. The error is later discovered, but neither the patient nor family ever learns of it. Which of the following most accurately describes this situation?
a: No liability because the error was unintentional.
No liability because the pharmacy should have caught the error.
No liability because the error is unknown to the patient.
No liability because it was an accident.
The resident and hospital are both liable for harm to the patient.
No liability because there was no permanent harm.
1_medical_ethics.l: Malpractice exists when there is: A preventable error in care. Resulting harm to the patient. Intent is irrelevant — negligence causing harm constitutes liability even if accidental or undisclosed. Both the individual (resident) and the institution (vicarious liability) share responsibility.
1_medical_ethics.h: Malpractice (Unintentional Error by Staff Leading to Harm)

1_medical_ethics.q: Which of the following most closely represents the role of risk management in the hospital?
a: To ensure proper ethical management of patients.
To ensure proper clinical care of patients.
To act as a patient advocate.
To clarify communications from the medical staff to the patient.
To minimize the legal risk to the hospital from litigation.
1_medical_ethics.l: Risk management is an administrative function focused on identifying, analyzing, and mitigating potential legal exposures. It does not manage direct patient care or ethics decisions but instead works to prevent and defend against lawsuits and protect the institution’s legal interests.

1_medical_ethics.q: You explained the risks and benefits of bone marrow transplantation versus chemotherapy to a woman with leukemia. This was part of an IRB-approved trial. You told her that the transplant has the highest chance of cure of the disease but also the highest risk of immediate death. She understood what you said and signed the consent for transplantation. She dies as a result of the bone marrow transplant and her husband sues you for wrongful death. What will be the most likely outcome?
a: He loses the lawsuit because BMT is the standard of care for this patient's age and disease.
He wins because he was not informed.
He wins because chemotherapy has less risk of death.
He loses it because the patient was fully informed about the risks and benefits of both alternatives.
He loses because the trial was IRB-approved.
1_medical_ethics.l: The key issue in liability for adverse effects is whether the patient was fully informed of the risks of the therapy before treatment was given. If a major adverse effect or even death occurred, but the patient knew and understood this and still chose to proceed, there is no liability. In this case, the patient was informed of the risks of both the transplant and the alternative (chemotherapy) and chose the transplant anyway. IRB approval alone does not shield a physician from liability, but informed consent does.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 57-year-old corporate executive comes to the emergency department with an ST segment elevation myocardial infarction. He receives thrombolytics but has persistent chest pain. The patient asks you about the risks and benefits of angioplasty. You tell him he could have a hematoma or a coronary rupture as an adverse effect of the angioplasty balloon. You tell him that the benefit will be that it stops the chest pain. He opts for further medical management without angioplasty because of fear of adverse effects. He dies and his estate sues you. What will be the most likely outcome?
a: You win because you fully informed him of the adverse effects of the procedure.
You win because he refused the treatment offered.
You lose because patients cannot refuse lifesaving therapy.
You lose because you should have done the angioplasty before the thrombolytics.
You lose because you did not inform the patient of the serious consequences of forgoing angioplasty.
1_medical_ethics.l: In order for a patient to make an informed consent (or refusal), they must be informed of the risk of not having the procedure done. The physician must inform the patient not only of the risks of the treatment but also what will happen if they do NOT undergo the procedure (i.e., increased risk of death in an MI without reperfusion). The physician is liable because this information was not documented or given, meaning the refusal was not fully informed.
1_medical_ethics.h: Informed Consent (Failure to Disclose Risks of Forgoing Therapy)

1_medical_ethics.q: A 72-year-old woman is admitted to the hospital with gastroenteritis and dehydration. There are no indications in the chart of her having dizziness. She also does not directly inform the nurses of her dizziness. She asks to be brought to the bathroom. She is left alone on the toilet when she becomes lightheaded, faints, and hits her head against a wall sustaining a laceration. The patient and her family subsequently file suit against the hospital for negligence. What will be the most likely outcome?
a: She wins secondary to the negligence of the nurses.
She wins secondary to the negligence of the physicians.
She wins if she has sustained significant brain damage.
She loses because there was no significant injury.
She loses because she did not inform the nurses she was dizzy or lightheaded.
1_medical_ethics.l: Malpractice occurs if the staff deviated from the standard of care AND the patient sustained harm. It is not a deviation from the standard of care to leave a patient unescorted on the toilet if she does not offer a complaint. The patient has a responsibility to inform staff of medical conditions that would have an impact on her care. If she had informed the nurses she was dizzy and in need of assistance, and they failed to provide it, then malpractice would have occurred.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 70-year-old man is admitted to the hospital with endocarditis. At seven days of therapy the antibiotic order expires and you forget to reorder the medication for two days in the middle of a four-week course. There is no clinical deterioration and the antibiotics are restarted. What should you tell the patient?
a: Tell the patient it was a clerical error.
Because it was not clinically significant you do not have to say anything to the patient.
Apologize and tell the patient that you forgot to reorder the antibiotics, but that he will be all right.
Inform your chief resident but not the patient.
Ask the nurse to tell the patient about the error.
1_medical_ethics.l: The physician has a duty to inform patients of errors in care if those errors will have an impact on the patient's care or outcome. The patient has an absolute right to be fully informed about his own care, and his right to know is more important than your embarrassment. While there is no mandate to inform a patient about minor errors that will have no impact on her care (e.g., blood tests delayed), a two-day cessation of antibiotics for endocarditis is a sufficiently serious error to warrant disclosure, even if no harm was documented.

1_medical_ethics.q: You are a resident managing a private patient with cellulitis. The patient has a history of congestive heart failure and is on digoxin, an ACE inhibitor and a diuretic, but not a beta-blocker. You cannot find a contraindication to the use of beta-blockers. You ask the private attending why there is no beta-blocker and he says, "I have been in practice for 40 years. Don't you think I know what I am doing? Beta-blockers are dangerous in congestive failure". What should you do about this disagreement?
a: Wait for the attending to leave and give the patient a prescription for carvedilol.
Suggest to the patient that he should find another doctor.
Report the physician to the state licensing board.
Do nothing; he is the attending of record.
Bring the disagreement to the chief of service.
Help the patient find a good lawyer and volunteer to testify.
1_medical_ethics.l: When a resident disagrees with an attending on patient management, the resident's first duty is to the patient. However, the resident cannot unilaterally change therapy without the attending's knowledge (Option a). The next step is to appeal to the local hierarchy. Since this is a hospital patient, the appropriate authority is the Chief of Service. The Chief of Service has the authority to intervene in quality of care issues at the institution.

1_medical_ethics.q: A patient of yours has gone to the hospital to obtain a copy of her medical record for her own review. The hospital refuses to release them to her on the grounds that she must provide an adequate reason for wishing to see the records. She has come to see you to ask if this is true and how can she get her records. What should you tell her?
a: You, as the physician, can get the records for yourself to view, but she cannot.
The hospital will give her the records as long as you ask.
She has the right to have her own records as long as she has a legitimate reason.
She has the right to her own records even without giving a reason.
Only another physician, hospital, or insurance company can have free access to her records.
She can have the records as long as she has fully paid her hospital bills.
1_medical_ethics.l: The patient has an absolute right to free access to the information contained in her medical record, even though the physician/facility physically owns the paper/electronic record. The patient does not need to provide a reason for requesting her own property. Furthermore, the medical record cannot be held hostage for non-payment of bills.

1_medical_ethics.q: Which of the following most accurately describes the ownership of the medical record?
a: The record is entirely the property of the patient.
The record is entirely the property of the health-care provider.
The information is the property of the patient and the physical paper or electronic record is the property of the health-care provider.
The information is the property of the health-care provider and the physical paper or electronic record is the property of the patient.
1_medical_ethics.l: The physical medical record (paper or electronic) is legally the property of the healthcare provider/facility. However, the information contained within the record is the property of the patient, and they have an absolute right to access it.

1_medical_ethics.q: A man comes to the emergency department after a stab wound. Your notes document a 500-ml loss of blood. Later that night the patient develops asystole and dies. You find the loss of blood was originally really 3,000 ml, which was not recorded by you. What should you do to correct the documentation?
a: Use correction fluid to eliminate the original note.
Erase the original note.
Remove the original note from the chart.
Write a new note timing and dating it at the same time as the original note.
Write a new note with the current date and time.
1_medical_ethics.l: When an error in a chart needs correcting, you should never remove pages, use correction fluid, or erase the original note, as this makes it look like you are hiding errors. If you forgot to document something yesterday, you cannot 'back-date' notes. The correct method is to place an entirely new note with the current date and time and add the new information.

1_medical_ethics.q: The neighbor of a 14-year-old boy brings him to the emergency department after sustaining a laceration to the scalp from head trauma. You evaluate him and determine that suturing of the scalp will be necessary. Which of the following is the most accurate?
a: He is an emancipated minor; the patient can give consent.
The neighbor can give consent.
Wait for the consent of at least one parent.
Wait for the consent of both parents.
Seek a court-appointed legal guardian.
Consent is not necessary in this case.
1_medical_ethics.l: Consent is always implied for emergency treatment. Suturing a scalp laceration is considered necessary urgent care to prevent harm (infection, blood loss, scarring) that cannot wait for a parent or court to be located. Parental consent is implied in this emergency situation. Neighbors, aunts, uncles, or grandparents cannot give consent for a minor.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 15-year-old girl comes to the clinic for dyspareunia and a vaginal discharge. On speculum examination you find she has cervicitis. The infection is modest and there is no emergency. Which of the following is the most appropriate action to take?
a: Ceftriaxone/azithromycin now in a single dose.
Make at least a "good faith" effort to notify her parents and treat her.
Wait to inform at least one parent.
Treat only if the patient agrees to inform her parents.
Treat her now and inform the parents later.
1_medical_ethics.l: Minors are granted partial emancipation for issues concerning Sexually Transmitted Diseases (STDs), contraception, HIV, and prenatal care. Parental consent is not required to treat a minor for STDs. Society has an interest in preventing the spread of disease, and requiring consent would discourage minors from seeking treatment. Therefore, you should treat the patient immediately.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 12-year-old boy and his father are involved in a motor vehicle accident that results in a severe hemorrhage requiring an urgent blood transfusion. Both of them are clearly alert and understand that without the blood they may die. They are both Jehovah's Witnesses and are refusing blood transfusion on religious grounds. What should you do?
a: Honor their wishes; no transfusions for either one.
Obtain a court order to transfuse the child.
Give blood to the child but not to the father.
Psychiatric evaluation.
Use intravenous iron in both of them as a blood substitute.
Explain the situation to the child and ask him for consent for the transfusion.
1_medical_ethics.l: A competent adult (the father) has the right to refuse any medical care, even if it is lifesaving. However, the same right does not extend to parents concerning their children (minors). The state has an interest in the welfare of the child that exceeds the parental right to deny lifesaving therapy based on religious belief. You must give the blood to the minor to save his life.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 16-year-old female comes to the clinic after missing a period. Her pregnancy test is positive and she wants to start prenatal care. She is adamant that you keep the pregnancy confidential from her parents. What should you tell her?
a: "I will give you the care you need and keep it confidential."
"I will not mention it to your parents unless they ask. I can't lie."
"I am sorry, but I must tell them."
"I will not tell your parents, but I must inform the father of the baby."
1_medical_ethics.l: Prenatal care is one of the exceptions for which a minor is considered partially emancipated. Parental notification or consent is not required for prenatal care. The minor has a right to privacy, and confidentiality extends to protecting the patient from both her parents and the father of the baby.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 12-year-old girl presents with severe right lower-quadrant abdominal pain and marked tenderness and is found to have acute appendicitis. She is at a sleep-away camp. You are not able to locate her parents. The camp counselor and the director bring in the child. What should you do?
a: Do not do the surgery without parental consent.
Ask the camp counselor or director for consent.
Ask the patient for consent.
Perform the appendectomy.
Give intravenous antibiotics alone.
1_medical_ethics.l: Acute appendicitis is a medical emergency requiring urgent surgery. Consent is implied in an emergency for a minor (or anyone without capacity) when the parents are momentarily unreachable. You cannot allow harm to the child by delaying lifesaving surgery. Intravenous antibiotics alone are insufficient treatment for acute appendicitis.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 16-year-old female comes in for treatment of severe cystic acne with Accutane (isotretinoin). She lives alone and is self-supporting with a job as a waitress. She is aware of the potentially severe teratogenicity of isotretinoin. Her acne is severe and she still wishes for the isotretinoin. What should you tell her?
a: "This medication cannot be taken by women of reproductive age."
"Use benzoyl peroxide topically instead."
"I will give you the isotretinoin if your parents accompany you."
"I will give you the isotretinoin as requested."
"I will treat you with isotretinoin if you have a consent signed by your parents."
1_medical_ethics.l: A minor who is self-supporting, living independently, or married is considered an emancipated minor. An emancipated minor is considered capable of consenting to any medical procedure as if they were an adult. Therefore, parental consent is not required, and her decision to accept the treatment, understanding the risks, must be honored.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 65-year-old man comes to see you because he wants your help in committing suicide. He has metastatic colon cancer and is not in pain. He denies depression and seems to have a normal mood. He is asking for a prescription or combination of medications that he can take to end his life. Which of the following is most appropriate in this case?
a: Provide the pain medications as appropriate but not the means to end his life.
Provide the patient with medications he wants to end his life.
Have him undergo psychological screening first.
Refer him to a specialist in this area.
In-patient psychiatric evaluation for suicidal ideation.
Start antidepressants.
1_medical_ethics.l: Physician-assisted suicide (the physician providing the means for the patient to end their own life) is always considered unethical and unacceptable on the USMLE, even if local state law permits it. The physician's ethical imperative is to preserve life and relieve suffering. Therefore, you must continue to treat his pain but must not provide the means for him to commit suicide.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 79-year-old man comes to see you for assistance in ending his life. He has progressively worsening amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS). He finds his quality of life to be unacceptable and predicts he will become ventilator dependent. He is requesting that you administer a lethal injection in his home. He is not depressed, and his family is willing to honor his wishes. What should you tell him?
a: You tell him that you will honor his wish because he is competent and not depressed.
You tell him that you will honor his wish because his condition will worsen over time.
You agree to his wish because he has a right to a better quality of life.
You tell him that you cannot help him because there is no state law authorizing it.
You tell him that under no circumstances can you participate in euthanasia.
You say that it is okay as long as it happens in Oregon.
1_medical_ethics.l: Euthanasia (the physician administering the means of death) is illegal in all US states. It is ethically unacceptable for a physician to participate in any action that directly causes the death of a patient. The ethical duty to preserve life supersedes state law, the patient's request, and the patient's competence.

1_medical_ethics.q: You are a fourth-year medical student on a subinternship in obstetrics. You notice that the resident has come in with alcohol on his breath and some abnormal behavior. No one except you seems to notice. What should you do?
a: Nothing; you are subordinate to the resident.
Talk to the resident directly alone but don't mention it to the program director.
Tell the dean of students.
Report him to the state licensing board.
Report him to the chairperson or program director of his department.
Immediately inform his patients whom you feel are at risk.
1_medical_ethics.l: Physicians and students-in-training have a mandatory duty to report impaired physicians. You must report to the local supervisor who has authority over the impaired person. For a resident, this is the Program Director or Department Chair. Reporting provides a chance for confidential intervention and treatment for the resident.

1_medical_ethics.q: You are an attending physician at a university hospital. One of the attendings from another division seems to be having memory difficulty. You have found him twice in the hallway having forgotten where he was going. The residents tell you on the side that they don't rely on him at all. The chief of service knows but doesn't have enough attendings to fill the yearly schedule so he remains in place. What should you do?
a: Nothing; the chief of service already knows.
Talk to him directly.
Tell your division head.
Report him to the state licensing board.
1_medical_ethics.l: Reports of physician impairment should go first to the local supervisor. In this case, since the Chief of Service already knows and is allowing the impairment to continue (conflict of interest), the report must be made to a higher level such as the state licensing board. The impaired physician is a danger to patients.

1_medical_ethics.q: You are an attending at a large program in New York. You are out at a bar on Saturday night when you notice one of your residents in the same bar. She is clearly drunk. She is sitting behind you, too intoxicated to notice your presence. She is kissing a man who you know is not her husband. The following day you see her at the hospital managing patients and supervising interns. Her behavior and medical management seem completely normal. What should you do?
a: Speak to her directly about her behavior.
Discuss it with her program director or department chair.
Report her to the committee for physician impairment.
Do nothing.
1_medical_ethics.l: Your duty to report physician impairment rests only with reporting abnormalities that may affect patient care. If there is no evidence that the physician's personal life choices or weekend behavior are affecting her medical judgment, decision-making, or supervision of junior staff, then reporting it would only be potential slander.

1_medical_ethics.q: A physician in a busy inner-city environment has developed his practice over the years to the point where he no longer needs to solicit new patients. He instructs his office staff to begin refusing to accept new patients. Which of the following most appropriately describes his action?
a: It is both legal and ethical.
It is ethically acceptable, but illegal.
He is within his legal rights to refuse patients, but it is considered ethically unacceptable.
It is both illegal and unethical to refuse to accept new patients.
It is ethical as long as he arranges transfer of care to another physician.
1_medical_ethics.l: The relationship between a doctor and patient is voluntary and must be entered into freely by both parties. There is no legal or ethical mandate for a physician to accept a new patient, regardless of the patient's need or the physician's specialty, as long as the refusal is not based on arbitrary personal preferences (like race or gender).

1_medical_ethics.q: You are an employee of a managed-care plan (HMO). You have received written instructions not to bring up subjects such as bone marrow transplantation in myeloma patients because they are outrageously expensive. The data shows the procedure may extend survival. What do you do?
a: Fully inform the patient about the risks and benefits of bone marrow transplantation.
Refer your patient to an oncologist to have this discussion.
Transfer the patient to another primary-care provider.
Advise the patient to file suit against the managed care plan.
Give the patient treatment with melphalan or thalidomide which both are acceptable therapy.
