Inform the patient about bone marrow transplantation if he asks you about it.
1_medical_ethics.l: Your primary duty is always to the patient. The ethical boundaries of the doctor-patient relationship transcend the rules of the workplace or institutional rules. To obtain proper informed consent, the patient must be fully informed of all available treatment options and their risks and benefits. "Gag orders" are ethically wrong because they prevent patient autonomy.
1_medical_ethics.h: Doctor/Patient Relationship (HMO "Gag Order" on Treatment Options)

1_medical_ethics.q: You have a patient with severe coronary artery disease (3 vessels, <90% occlusion, LV dysfunction) referred for bypass surgery. The cardiothoracic surgeon at your hospital refuses to operate on your patient because he is scared of touching HIV-positive patients. What should you do?
a: Add calcium channel blockers.
Perform angioplasty and stenting.
Add clopidogrel.
Have his chief of service compel him to do the procedure.
Refer the patient to another cardiothoracic surgeon.
1_medical_ethics.l: Refusing care based on a patient's disease (HIV status) is generally unethical. However, a physician cannot be legally forced to take on a patient or perform a procedure against their will. Since the patient has a clear need for surgery (the right thing to do), and the consultant refuses, the correct ethical/procedural step is to find another doctor who will do the right thing.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 60-year-old male physician has had a female patient for the last 20 years. Both lost their spouses several years ago. They start spending time together outside the office. The female patient wants to begin a sexual/romantic relationship with the physician. What should he tell her?
a: "I can never do that with you, ever."
"We can be social, but not sexual."
"We need the ethics board's approval first."
"I cannot date you and be your doctor—maybe in the future we can date, after you get another doctor."
"Because this is your initiative we can begin dating."
1_medical_ethics.l: Sexual contact between a physician and a current patient is always inappropriate and unethical. To begin a personal relationship, the formal professional relationship must stop, and the patient's care must be transferred to another physician. The patient's initiative does not change the ethical prohibition.

1_medical_ethics.q: Which of the following does not need to be reported to the health department?
a: Salmonella.
Gonorrhea.
Herpes simplex.
Tuberculosis.
Measles.
1_medical_ethics.l: Diseases are reported to interrupt the cycle of transmission. Illnesses that are always reportable include AIDS, syphilis, tuberculosis, gonorrhea, and childhood diseases like measles. Herpes simplex is generally not reportable and there is no contact tracing for it because it cannot be eradicated and is dormant between outbreaks, so there is no utility in treating the contacts.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 38-year-old bus driver is seen in clinic for fever, cough, and sputum with an apical infiltrate as well as sputum positive for acid-fast bacilli. The patient is unwilling to take TB medications consistently and his sputum remains positive. Directly observed therapy (DOT) has failed, and he persists in his refusal. What should you do?
a: Nothing, he has a right to autonomy.
Arrest the patient and put him in prison.
Remove the family from the house.
Remove the patient from his job as a bus driver and incarcerate him in a hospital to take medications.
Physically restrain the patient and place a nasogastric tube to give the medications.
1_medical_ethics.l: A patient's right to privacy and autonomy ends when the public's right to safety is at risk. A patient with persistently positive sputum for acid-fast bacilli does not have the right to be at large, and thus incarceration in a hospital (not a jail) is legally permissible to prevent the spread of disease. Incarceration is the last resort to compel therapy. You cannot force-feed TB medications (Option e).

1_medical_ethics.q: A 27-year-old man is seen by you after the diagnosis of syphilis. As you are administering his treatment you find he is quite promiscuous. You inform him that you must notify the Department of Health and that his sexual contacts need to be treated. He is extremely embarrassed and asks how they will find out. What should you tell him?
a: You will notify them yourself but you will not give his name.
You will notify them and must let them know he was the contact.
You will tell the Department of Health but he himself must tell the contacts.
The Department of Health will send a letter or call the contacts and let them know they have a serious health issue. They will test and treat the partners but will not reveal his name.
He doesn't have a choice; he has to give the names.
Their individual doctors will inform the contacts.
1_medical_ethics.l: The primary purpose of reporting diseases like syphilis is to interrupt the transmission cycle while minimizing the breach of patient privacy. The Department of Health handles contact tracing and notification. They notify the partners that they have a serious health issue and offer testing and treatment. Crucially, they never reveal the name of the source patient (the patient's confidentiality is protected).

1_medical_ethics.q: You are the general internist in a large, multispecialty physician group. The office-based pregnancy test on your patient has just become positive and you estimate the gestational age of the fetus at eight weeks. You are a very deeply religious person …and you are opposed to abortion because you believe life begins at conception. After extensive discussion about the options, your patient asks to be referred for an abortion. Which of the following is the most appropriate action?
a: Inform the patient that you are morally opposed to abortion and you cannot make the referral.
Terminate your relationship with the patient as her physician.
Propose social work/psychological evaluation of the patient.
Tell her you will make the referral after a 30-day consideration period in which she may change her mind.
Refer the patient for the abortion.
1_medical_ethics.l: A physician is not obligated to perform a procedure that they find ethically objectionable (like abortion). However, once a doctor has entered into a patient relationship, they have an obligation to provide or arrange for the care the patient needs. You cannot refuse to refer the patient to another physician who will provide the requested, legal procedure. The patient has a legal right to an abortion within the first trimester.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 16-year-old female is in your office because she has just found out she is pregnant. She discusses her options and asks you to refer her for an abortion. She states that her parents do not know she is pregnant and she does not want them to know. What should you do?
a: Refer her for the abortion without parental notification.
Make a "reasonable effort" to contact the parents, but still refer for the abortion if you cannot contact them.
Do not refer for abortion without parental consent.
Seek a court order declaring her emancipated.
Strongly encourage her to discuss the issue with her parents.
1_medical_ethics.l: The rules regarding parental consent/notification for a minor's abortion are mixed nationally. Because the USMLE is a national exam, there is no single right legal answer. The consistently correct ethical first step is to encourage voluntary disclosure to the parents.

1_medical_ethics.q: A young husband and wife visit your office to discuss methods of contraception. The wife decides that she wants a tubal ligation for sterilization. Her husband is shocked and strongly objects. His wife is clear that she wants the sterilization and wishes to be referred for the procedure. What should you do?
a: Refer for the tubal ligation as requested by the wife.
Refer after a psychological evaluation.
Do not make the referral because she is young.
Do not make the referral because she may change her mind.
Refer only with the husband's consent.
1_medical_ethics.l: A person has absolute autonomy over their own body. Spousal consent is NOT required for sterilization or any other reproductive procedure (like abortion or contraception). Since the fallopian tubes are located within the woman's body, her wishes alone determine the procedure.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 35-year-old married woman with four children is in your office seeking a termination of an unwanted pregnancy in the first trimester. Later, her husband calls and is very distressed because he does not want the abortion to occur and has a name picked out. What should you tell him?
a: You will try to talk his wife out of the abortion.
You will cancel the procedure immediately.
You say you cannot hold up the termination without a court order.
You cannot prevent the abortion without paternity testing confirming that he is the father.
You say you have an absolute duty to the mother to honor her wishes no matter what his personal feelings are.
1_medical_ethics.l: The fetus is considered an extension of the mother's body until delivery, and she has absolute autonomy. Paternal consent is NOT required for an abortion, even if the father objects strongly. The physician's absolute duty is to the pregnant woman.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 30-year-old woman presents to the clinic during her third trimester (estimated gestational age of the fetus is 28 weeks) and she is seeking an abortion. The patient is generally healthy. Routine testing showed no abnormalities. What should you tell the patient?
a: It's okay; you will go ahead with the abortion.
You will be happy to comply if she can get a court order.
No way, third-trimester abortions are prohibited.
Legally you can only do it if her life is at risk.
No, you can't do it because the fetus is normal.
1_medical_ethics.l: Abortion during the third trimester (when the fetus is potentially viable) is the most controversial area of reproductive rights. Third-trimester abortions are clearly restricted. Legally, abortions at this stage are usually permitted only if the life of the mother is at risk, or if the fetus has a severe, abnormal condition.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 42-year-old man comes to see you for routine management. He has multiple scratches and contusions and says his wife routinely abuses him. He very clearly states that he does not want these injuries reported. What do you tell him?
a: You have no choice but to report the injuries because you are a mandatory reporter.
You will report the injury only with his consent.
You will honor his wish but must report it if there is another episode.
There is no spousal abuse reporting.
You will report it if you find evidence that the wife was really the attacker.
1_medical_ethics.l: The rules on reporting domestic violence (spousal abuse) are less strict than those for child or elder abuse. Since the victim is a competent adult, their right to autonomy means the physician cannot violate the patient's refusal to report the injury. The physician's duty is to encourage reporting and offer counseling, but not to act against the patient's express wishes.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 68-year-old man attempted suicide by driving his car into a telephone pole with the intentional purpose of ending his life. He is severely hemorrhaging and in the emergency department he refuses to give his consent for surgery. He states he wants to die, having been recently diagnosed with cancer. What should you do about the surgery to stop the bleeding now?
a: Follow his stated wish and withhold surgery.
Perform the surgery.
Obtain a court order to force the surgery.
Ask the family members for consent.
1_medical_ethics.l: A patient who is actively trying to kill himself is, by definition, not considered competent (lacking capacity) because of impaired judgment. Therefore, his refusal of lifesaving care is not an informed refusal. The right to autonomy ends just short of condoning active suicide. In an emergency, the physician must intervene to prevent active suicide and provide lifesaving care immediately.

1_medical_ethics.q: You are a civilian physician and you have been asked to participate in the interrogation of a prisoner suspected of carrying out a terrorist attack. You have been asked to monitor the patient's oxygen level during a simulated "hanging" and "strangulation" of the patient. What should you do?
a: It is ethically permissible to participate.
Participation is permissible only if he has been convicted of the crime.
You can participate if there is a court order.
You must have a release signed by the military prior to your participation.
You cannot participate in the purposeful torture of a prisoner.
1_medical_ethics.l: Physicians are never ethically allowed to participate in torture at any level. The physician's duty to the patient transcends duty to ordinary institutional structure. This is true even if there is a court order. Monitoring vital signs during torture is a form of participation that supports harm.

1_medical_ethics.q: A group of prisoners is brought to a military hospital in which you work. You notice that several of the patients have burns that look like cigarettes were put out on them as well as several broken bones that have started to heal. What should you do?
a: Report the injuries as signs of possible torture.
Report the injuries only on the patients to be released.
If you are a military physician, you should report the injuries.
If you are a civilian physician, you should report the injuries.
Your only duty is to treat the injuries.
1_medical_ethics.l: You have a duty to protect the welfare of all patients, including prisoners. Failing to report injuries that are evidence of abuse or torture makes you an accomplice, and this ethical duty transcends your presence either in or out of the military. You are a mandated reporter of child abuse, elder abuse, and injuries consistent with torture.

1_medical_ethics.q: An elderly patient is in a persistent vegetative state secondary to anoxic encephalopathy and now develops multiorgan failure, including renal failure. Renal failure develops to the point of needing dialysis but you feel the dialysis would be completely futile. Which of the following is the most appropriate step in management?
a: Hemodialysis.
Peritoneal dialysis.
Renal transplantation.
Give albumin.
Recommend that dialysis not be performed.
1_medical_ethics.l: The physician is not obligated to provide treatment that is futile. In this case, since the patient has permanent, severe brain damage and multi-organ failure, dialysis would not prolong meaningful life and would only prolong the dying process. Withholding futile care is ethical, even if the family requests it.
1_medical_ethics.h: Futile Care (Dialysis for Persistent Vegetative State)

1_medical_ethics.q: A 33-year-old female boxer sustains a cervical spine fracture resulting in paralysis and is ventilator dependent. She is fully alert and understands her condition. There is no hope of recovery. She is repeatedly and clearly requesting removal from the ventilator. What should you do?
a: Remove the ventilator as she requests.
Obtain a court order to continue the ventilator.
Seek family consensus on removing the ventilator.
Seek approval of the health-care proxy.
Sedate the patient and continue the ventilator.
1_medical_ethics.l: A competent adult has the right to refuse or withdraw any medical treatment, including mechanical ventilation. There is no ethical or legal distinction between withholding and withdrawing care. Since the patient has full capacity, her wishes must be honored immediately. The family's opinion or a court order is not required.
1_medical_ethics.h: Withdrawal of Treatment (Competent Adult on Ventilator)

1_medical_ethics.q: A 48-year-old woman has developed stage III non-Hodgkin's lymphoma. Without chemotherapy she has no hope of survival beyond a few weeks. With therapy she has an 80% chance of complete remission. She understands this but insists that she simply does not want the therapy. There is no evidence of depression. Which of the following is the most appropriate action?
a: Psychiatric evaluation.
Ask the family for their opinion.
Seek a court-appointed guardian.
Honor the patient's wishes.
Offer radiotherapy instead.
Risk management evaluation.
1_medical_ethics.l: A competent adult has the right to refuse any medical therapy, even if that therapy is lifesaving. Because the patient is alert, understands the risks, and is not depressed, her autonomy overrides the physician's desire to provide beneficence. No one, including a court or family, can overrule a competent patient's refusal of treatment.

1_medical_ethics.q: An elderly patient with progressive Parkinson disease comes to see you because of fever and symptoms consistent with pneumonia. His Parkinson disease has been worsening and he has become quite depressed (insomnia, early morning waking, weight loss, anhedonia). He is refusing antibiotics and is asking for palliative care only to help him die. What should you do?
a: Psychiatric evaluation
Sedate the patient
Comply with the patient's wishes
Seek the opinion of the family
Ethics committee referral
Comply with the patient's wishes as long as he is DNR
1_medical_ethics.l: A patient's refusal of lifesaving therapy must be an informed and voluntary choice made by a competent person. Depression with vegetative signs is a temporary condition that impairs a patient's judgment, meaning their refusal may not be fully competent. You must evaluate and treat the depression first before honoring a refusal of a potentially lifesaving treatment like antibiotics for pneumonia.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 55-year-old man is admitted for worsening mental status. He has Creutzfeld-Jakob disease. He repeatedly told you he does not want to be "kept alive as a vegetable". His health-care proxy form specifically states "no placement of a nasogastric or gastric tube for enteral feeding". The proxy (a nurse) insists you place a jejunostomy (J-tube), telling you the proxy form only excludes NG and G-tubes. What should you do?
a: Place the J-tube.
Tell the proxy she needs to get a court order for the J-tube.
Tell the proxy she needs an ethics committee evaluation.
Do not place any form of tube for artificial nutrition or hydration.
Transfer the patient's care to another physician who feels comfortable placing the J-tube.
1_medical_ethics.l: The patient gave a clear oral advance directive ("does not want to be kept alive as a vegetable") and a written directive explicitly refusing feeding tubes. Although the written form is slightly vague (excluding NG and G-tubes), the patient's verbal and written wishes that he not be maintained on any artificial nutrition must be considered the absolute rule and upheld. The proxy's job is to carry out the patient's wishes, not overrule them based on a technicality.
1_medical_ethics.h: Refusal of Artificial Nutrition (Proxy Disagrees with Vague Form)

1_medical_ethics.q: A 35-year-old man is a victim of a motor vehicle accident and is being considered for endotracheal intubation. His mother and his domestic partner are present. The domestic partner is the health-care agent (proxy). The agent states unequivocally that the patient stated both verbally and in writing that he wished never to be placed on a "breathing machine for any purpose". What should you do?
a: Honor the proxy and do not intubate.
Honor the mother's wishes and intubate the patient.
Consult the ethics committee.
Neurology evaluation.
Try intubation for 24–48 hours to see if he recovers.
Ask the opinion of the rest of the family.
1_medical_ethics.l: A Health-Care Proxy is the ultimate decision maker in health care once the patient loses capacity. The proxy's authority overrules all other potential decision-makers (including the mother). Since the patient's wishes against the specific intervention (a breathing machine) were clearly known to the proxy, those wishes must be honored immediately.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 75-year-old man is admitted with a myocardial infarction and stroke leaving him in a persistent vegetative state. He never designated a health-care proxy or left a written will. His nephew and daughter want to continue all forms of therapy. His son and brother want to stop everything. Both parties believe they know the patient's wishes. What should you do?
a: Encourage discussion amongst the family.
You, the physician, make the decision in the best interest of the patient.
Stop all forms of therapy.
Obtain a court order seeking a court-appointed guardian.
1_medical_ethics.l: When there is no clear advance directive (proxy or living will) and the family is in disagreement (and thus cannot provide a united oral advance directive), the first and most appropriate action is always to try to seek consensus by encouraging discussion or a family meeting. If consensus cannot be reached, the next step is ethics committee consultation.

1_medical_ethics.q: An 84-year-old woman with severe Alzheimer's disease is admitted to your skilled-nursing facility. She has lost the ability to communicate. She did not appoint a proxy and there is no clear verbal advance directive. Multiple family members visit her, but you are unable to achieve a clear consensus amongst the family of what the patient's wishes were. What should you do in terms of her care?
a: Follow the wishes of the eldest child.
Follow what you think is best.
Ask another attending physician's opinion.
Ask the hospital administrator for consent.
Pursue an ethics committee evaluation.
1_medical_ethics.l: When there is no clear evidence of the patient's wishes (no proxy, no living will, no family consensus), you must seek the "best substituted judgment". If family consensus is impossible, the case must be referred to the hospital ethics committee to seek consensus and weigh the evidence. This is followed only by referral to the courts if the ethics committee fails.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 72-year-old man has severe pain from metastatic prostate cancer. He needs more intense pain management with subcutaneous or intravenous opiates. He also has severe COPD, and the staff is concerned that increasing his pain medications will decrease his respiratory drive. The patient is fully alert and understands that the medications may shorten his life inadvertently. Which of the following is the most ethical way to approach his pain management?
a: It is all right as long as he is DNR.
It is acceptable as long as the patient understands the risks.
It is unacceptable to shorten life with physician-administered medications.
Intubate the patient then give the pain medications.
Leave him in pain as long as the respiratory drive is not impaired.
Offer to end his life with pain medications.
1_medical_ethics.l: This is the principle of "Double Effect". The primary ethical duty is to relieve suffering. If the primary intent is to relieve intractable pain, and the unintended adverse effect is the shortening of life, the treatment is ethically acceptable. The patient must be informed of the risk, and since he is competent and understands, the aggressive pain management is warranted.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 77-year-old woman is admitted with a stroke that renders the patient dependent on a ventilator. There is no hope of recovery and she is unable to communicate. There is no health-care proxy and no family is available. Her husband produces a living will that says, "I do not wish to be maintained on a ventilator if there is no hope of recovery". What should you do regarding the ventilator and tube feeding?
a: Continue both for now.
Remove the ventilator and the tube feeding.
Remove the nasogastric tube but continue the ventilator.
Seek a court-appointed guardian.
Ask the rest of the family what they think.
1_medical_ethics.l: A living will is only effective if it is clear and specific. The phrase "no heroic measures" is vague and not sufficiently clear to terminate life support. Since there is no clear directive, and no proxy/family to act as a surrogate, you must err on the side of preserving life and continue all necessary therapy (the default position) until a clearer decision-making path emerges.

1_medical_ethics.q: An 84-year-old woman becomes febrile, severely hypotensive, and tachycardic from an intestinal perforation. She is disoriented with no capacity. She has no health-care proxy, but the family is in uniform agreement on what the patient would have wanted for herself. Which of the following cannot be stopped at the direction of the family?
a: Ventilator.
Blood tests.
Dopamine.
Fluid and nutrition.
Nothing.
1_medical_ethics.l: There is no legal or ethical distinction between withholding and withdrawing care. When a patient loses capacity, but the family is united in agreement about what the patient would have wanted (oral advance directive), they can stop any form of treatment, including the ventilator, dopamine, blood tests, or artificial nutrition. Since all of the listed therapies are medical treatments, none are prohibited from being withdrawn/stopped (the correct answer is "Nothing" cannot be stopped).
1_medical_ethics.h: Withdrawal of Care (Family Consensus, Irreversible Condition)

1_medical_ethics.q: A 73-year-old man is at a chronic-care facility on a ventilator. He is fully alert and understands the situation. He is asking to have the nasogastric tube removed because of discomfort. What should you tell him?
a: "I will get that tube out right away, sir."
"Let's see how much you are able to eat first."
"I will pull it out if you let me put in a gastric tube."
"Let me talk to your family first."
"Do you have a health-care proxy?"
"No way dude, we make the decisions around here, not you."
"Only if you promise not to sue me."
"Are you crazy? Do you think I am going to let MY patient starve to death?"
1_medical_ethics.l: The artificial administration of fluids and nutrition (like an NG tube) is considered a medical procedure/treatment. A competent adult has the right to stop any form of therapy they wish. You cannot legally force-feed a patient against their will. Since he is alert and competent, his wish to have the tube removed must be honored immediately.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 47-year-old man with end-stage renal failure has asked you to stop his dialysis. The patient fully understands that he will die if he stops dialysis. He is not depressed. What should you tell him?
a: "I need a court order first."
"I am sorry; I don't feel comfortable doing that."
"I can't do that. Physician-assisted suicide is not ethical."
"I will stop when we get you a kidney transplant."
"You will feel better if I sedate you so that you stop disagreeing with me."
"Although I disagree with your decision, I will stop the dialysis."
"I cannot do that. We already started dialysis. Now we have to continue."
"Not until you pay your bill."
1_medical_ethics.l: A competent adult has the absolute right to stop any medical therapy, even if that means death. There is no ethical or legal distinction between withholding dialysis and withdrawing it after it has started. Stopping dialysis is NOT physician-assisted suicide; the intent is to honor the patient's autonomy, and the cause of death is the underlying disease, not a lethal injection.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 92-year-old man with Alzheimer's is on a ventilator and nasogastric tube. He lacks capacity. You find an old living will that says "No heroic measures," "I want to be DNR," and "I wish to be kept comfortable". There is no proxy and no family is available. What should you do regarding the ventilator and tube feeding?
a: Continue both for now.
Remove the ventilator and the tube feeding.
Remove the nasogastric tube but continue the ventilator.
Seek a court-appointed guardian.
Decide what you think is best for the patient.
1_medical_ethics.l: The phrase "No heroic measures" is too vague to serve as a clear directive to remove a ventilator or tube feeding, which are considered ordinary care for a patient in this state. When a directive is not clear, and there is no proxy or family available to clarify the patient's wishes, you must continue life support (the default position) until the situation is clarified.

1_medical_ethics.q: Which of the following best approximates the role of the health-care proxy?
a: To explain the physician's plan to the family.
To convey the wishes of the family to the medical team.
To communicate and carry out the wishes of the patient.
To make decisions based on what he or she genuinely believes is the best interests of the patient.
To carry out the financial decisions of the patient when he or she loses the capacity to speak.
1_medical_ethics.l: The health-care proxy is the patient's agent or messenger, appointed to communicate and enforce the patient's previously expressed wishes for medical treatment in the event they lose capacity. The proxy is not a financial executor and is not there to impose their own idea of the patient's best interests over the patient's known wishes.

1_medical_ethics.q: A 34-year-old woman with three children presents pregnant at 20 weeks of gestational age with anemia and severe gastrointestinal bleeding requiring transfusion to save her life. She is a Jehovah's Witness and she is refusing blood transfusion. What should you do?
a: Honor her wishes, no transfusion.
No transfusion now, but the transfusion is mandatory after the second trimester when the fetus is a potentially viable baby.
Transfuse the patient.
Seek a court order mandating the transfusion.
Get consent of the father of the baby.
1_medical_ethics.l: A competent adult (the mother) has the absolute right to refuse any treatment, even if it is lifesaving. The fetus (at 20 weeks) is not a legal person and does not have independent rights; the mother's autonomy is paramount. Therefore, the correct answer is to honor her refusal (Option a). The physician must respect her autonomy while offering supportive care.

1_medical_ethics.q: Mr. Barber is a 58-year-old man who has a cardiac arrest after surgery. He suffers permanent brain damage and is in a persistent vegetative state and ventilator dependent. His wife and eight children request in writing for the ventilator to be stopped. They agree that this was his wish for himself, but he did not leave a written advance directive. What should you do?
a: Refer the case to the ethics committee.
Obtain a court order.
Refuse; you cannot ethically do this.
Transfer the care of the patient to another physician.
Remove the IV lines and stop blood draws as they wish.
You can withhold additional new treatments and tests, but you cannot stop those already started.
1_medical_ethics.l: When there is no formal advance directive but the family is united in testifying to the patient's wishes (an oral advance directive), those wishes are binding, and withdrawal of care is permissible. Since the family agrees this was his wish, and there is no hope of recovery (PVS), the principle of autonomy allows the withdrawal of all medical support. The IV lines and blood draws, like the ventilator, are medical treatments that the patient had the right to refuse.
1_medical_ethics.h: Withdrawal of Care (Oral Advance Directive, No Proxy)
